title: Vodacom (Pty) Limited v Kenneth Nkosana Makate and Another
text:
THE CONSTITUTIONAL COURT OF SOUTH AFRICA 
Case Number: CCT51/2024 
SCA Case number: 401/2022                            
High Court Case number: 57882/2019 
 
In the matter between: 
VODACOM PTY LTD Applicant 
and 
NKOSANA KENNETH MAKATE First Respondent 
SHAMEEL JOOSUB N.O. Second Respondent 
 
 
MR MAKATE’S HEADS OF ARGUMENT  
 
TABLE OF CONTENTS

THIS COURT’S APPEAL JURISDICTION HAS NOT BEEN TRIGGERED BY VODACOM .................. 8

VODACOM’S APPLICATION FOR LEAVE TO APPEAL: A VEILED APPEAL ON FACT ................. 11 
THE “JURISDICTION” POINT HAS NO MERIT ......................................................................................... 12 
THE COMPLAINT THAT SUBSTITUTION WAS NOT AN ISSUE BEFORE THE SCA IS UNSUSTAINABLE ............... 12 
COSTS IS ALWAYS A DISCRETIONARY REMEDY .......................................................................................... 15 
VODACOM’S IMPERMISSIBLE NEW COMPLAINT ABOUT THE APPLICATION OF THE PRINCIPLES OF 
SUBSTITUTION ....................................................................................................................................... 15 
GROUND 2: THE SCA ORDERS ARE NOT VAGUE .................................................................................... 17 
  ii 
GROUND 3: THE MAJORITY DID NOT “DISREGARD” ISSUES AND EVIDENCE ............................. 18

VODACOM’S LACK OF EVIDENCE ON KEY ISSUES IN DISPUTE ................................................................... 22 
THE CEO’S EX POST FACTO REASONS AND FACTS WERE CORRECTLY DISREGARDED ................................ 24

THERE IS NO REAL DISPUTE REGARDING THE PATENT OMISSION OF MTR AND CONTRACT SUBSCRIBERS .. 32

  1 
INTRODUCTION AND OVERVIEW 
1 This Court has set Vodacom’s application for leave to appeal down for a hearing on 21 November 
2024. On 21 November 2000 – 24 years ago to the day – Mr Makate first presented his ‘Please 
Call Me’ invention (“PCM”) to Vodacom. Despite a protracted legal battle to establish the 
existence of a contract and his entitlement to a share in the revenue generated by PCM, Vodacom 
continues to resist Mr Makate’s claims.  
2 Mr Makate, then a young trainee accountant at Vodacom, came up with the genius invention, 
which Vodacom – the time – described as “a world first” and recognised that it was all “thanks to 
Kenneth Makate”.1 Mr Makate was promised compensation in the form of a share in revenue. 
3 It is common cause that Vodacom has earned billions of Rands from Mr Makate’s invention. 
Mr Makate does not suggest that his claim does not involve a large sum of money. Whatever 
adverse description Vodacom ascribes to Mr Makate’s share of revenue – it must be kept in mind 
that his share is just 5% – and all parties accept that 5% is a fair share. Vodacom has already made 
and kept 95% of the money generated. Mr Makate’s entitlement flows from a proven contract. As 
we show below, common cause facts show that 38% of calls are responded to within the first 24 
hours of a PCM being sent. In terms of Mr Makate’s model, Mr Makate only seeks 5% of the 
revenue generated from calls returned in response to PCMs within the first hour. Vodacom keeps 
95% of that revenue. And Vodacom keeps 100% of the revenue for any calls responding to PCMs 
after the first hour.  
4 Despite the product being such an overwhelming success, Vodacom refused to negotiate 
compensation for the use of Mr Makate’s invention.2 After various attempts by Mr Makate to 
negotiate with Vodacom on his share were rebuked, Mr Makate turned to the Courts. The various 
judgments make clear that for most of the last 24 years, Vodacom has sought to escape paying 
 
1 Makate v Vodacom Ltd 2016 (4) SA 121 (CC) (“Makate Constitutional Court judgment”) at para 6. 
2 Makate Constitutional Court judgment at para 11. 
  2 
Mr Makate anything. It even accused Mr Makate of stealing the invention from MTN.3 
5 Notwithstanding scathing reviews by the various Courts, Vodacom persists in seeking to minimise 
Mr Makate’s contribution (even before this Court).  The R47 million awarded by the CEO is a 
fraction of the billions of Rand that Vodacom has raked in from Mr Makate’s invention since 
March 2001.4  
6 The High Court and majority of the SCA – quite correctly – recognised this and the SCA 
majority’s order, at long last, provides Mr Makate with compensation that amounts to 5% of the 
revenue earned from PCM for a period of 18 years. Vodacom’s application for leave to appeal to 
this Court is a further attempt to deny Mr Makate what he is owed.  
7 In summary, and as elaborated below, we submit that Vodacom’s application for leave to appeal 
should be dismissed for six main reasons. 
8 First, it fails to meet the jurisdictional requirements for leave to appeal.  
8.1 It raises no constitutional issue, nor any arguable point of law of general public importance. 
8.2 Nor, we submit, would it be in the interests of justice for this Court to stand in the way of 
Mr Makate, finally, receiving compensation for his invention after a 24-year struggle with 
Vodacom. 
9 Second, the present dispute is a purely private commercial matter in which this Court has already 
found that Mr Makate is contractually entitled to be paid a share of the revenue generated by his 
invention. The present issue is about quantifying Mr Makate’s share of the revenue – nothing 
more. Vodacom seeks to characterise what is only a factual dispute as raising a “jurisdictional” and 
“constitutional” issues. That characterisation is untenable. It holds out the spectre for all 
disagreements about factual conclusions as going to jurisdiction and hence being matters for this 
 
3 Makate Constitutional Court judgment at para 13. 
4 Mr Makate’s FA at Vol 1 p 10 para 10. It is in fact merely 0.023% of the R205 billion in call revenue that Mr Makate’s 
team calculated that the PCM earned Vodacom from 2001 to 2020. That figure does not consider the revenue that 
Vodacom will continue to earn from PCM going forward.  
  3 
Court. 
10 Third, Vodacom’s application for leave to appeal is based only on this Court’s constitutional 
jurisdiction. Vodacom claims that the majority judgment violated its right to a fair hearing under 
section 34 of the Constitution.5 But there is a central flaw in the logic of Vodacom’s argument. Fair 
hearing rights are procedural in nature and concern the conduct and procedure of the hearing.6  
Section 34 affords a party a right to a fair hearing, not a right to a perfect, or even correct, 
judgment. As this court has explained: “the Constitution does not and could hardly ensure that 
litigants are protected against wrong decisions.”7 
11 Fourth, the three bases on which Vodacom alleges its section 34 right was infringed are without 
merit.  
11.1 Firstly, Vodacom claims that the majority of the SCA exceeded its jurisdiction by 
considering the issues of substitution and costs in the absence of a cross-appeal by 
Mr Makate. Vodacom claimed that the majority’s options were only: to uphold Vodacom’s 
appeal or to dismiss it. But Vodacom’s complaint in the application for leave to appeal was 
premised on an incorrect reading of the majority of the SCA’s judgment and order. 
Vodacom claimed that because its appeal had been dismissed, it was not open to consider 
the issues of remedy and costs again. Vodacom’s heads of argument, however, now accept 
(correctly with respect) that Vodacom’s appeal was partially successful. Once that was so it 
was entirely open to the majority of the SCA to revisit the discretionary issues of the 
appropriate remedy and costs.  
11.2 Secondly, Vodacom’s contention that the SCA’s order is vague is incorrect and based on a 
blinkered reading of the judgment. Properly interpreted, in the light of the judgment and 
pleadings, the order is clear. The patent typographical errors in the order plainly do not 
 
5 Vodacom’s heads of argument paras 85-86.  
6 De Beer NO v North-Central Local Council and South-Central Local Council and Others (Umhlatuzana Civic Association 
Intervening) 2002 (1) SA 429 (CC) at para 15. 
7 Lane and Fey NNO v Dabelstein and Others 2001 (2) SA 1187 (CC) at para 4; Eke v Parsons 2016 (3) SA 37 (CC) at para 
48. 
  4 
raise any constitutional issue, nor found jurisdiction for this Court to hear the matter. That 
is particularly so given Mr Makate’s express commitment – under oath – given before this 
Court in his answering affidavit – regarding the correct interpretation of the order (and his 
abandonment of the portions with typographical errors).  Put differently, Mr Makate will be 
held to the order as properly interpreted and what he has confined his claim to. There is thus 
no potential prejudice to Vodacom (as it claims). This ground is dealt with in further detail 
in Mr Makate’s answering affidavit and given Vodacom’s approach of raising a laundry list 
of baseless factual disputes and complaints against the SCA majority, we only deal with 
this ground of appeal briefly below.  
11.3 Thirdly, Vodacom says that orders made by Mocumie JA, Mothle JA and Nhlangulela AJA 
were: “the product of the majority’s ignorance or disregard of the true issues in the case 
and the evidence relevant to them”;8 and because the right to a fair hearing under section 34 
of the Constitution protects litigants against “orders carelessly made in incomprehensible 
terms”.9 
11.3.1 But the thrust of Vodacom’s application for leave to appeal rests on its 
disagreement with the SCA’s factual findings. This Court has made clear that 
disagreements with a court below do not raise any constitutional issue.10 In 
essence, Vodacom invites this Court to cross-check references from affidavits 
and come to different factual conclusions. With respect, that is not the task of 
the highest Court in the land.  
11.3.2 In any event, the alleged factual errors are not errors at all. The majority’s 
central findings are entirely consistent with the evidence presented. In truth, 
there was a striking lack of evidence presented by Vodacom.   
12 Fifth, Vodacom’s “interests of justice” section seeks to introduce new evidence that did not form 
 
8 Vodacom’s Application for Leave to Appeal (CC) at Vol 25 p 2575 para 8.  
9 Vodacom’s Application for Leave to Appeal (CC) at Vol 25 p 2575 para 39.  
10 For instance, in S v Boesak 2001 (1) SA 912 (CC). 
  5 
any part of Vodacom’s case or evidence before the High Court or before the SCA. For instance,  
Vodacom at no stage alleged that it would have difficulty paying the amount claimed by 
Mr Makate.  
12.1 Vodacom has not applied to introduce the new evidence before this Court on appeal. Nor 
has Vodacom made out any case for direct access for this Court to consider that evidence as 
a court of first and final instance. Vodacom has plainly elected not to do so because it 
appreciates that it could never satisfy the high threshold set by this Court.  
12.2 In any event, the entire interests of justice section states that Vodacom will suffer a series of 
ill effects if the majority order is imposed and if the amount of the quantum is in the order 
of R40 billion.11 Underneath that amount, Vodacom will not suffer the drastic effects. But 
the quantum, as Mr Makate stated on oath before this Court in his answering affidavit, 
while still substantial, is an order of magnitude less – at R9,4 billion.  
12.3 It is opportunistic that Vodacom should seek to raise its “concern” on the effect that the 
SCA’s order will have on its Black-economic empowerment partner, Yebo Yethu, when:  
12.3.1 for the last 24 years Vodacom has attempted to escape paying a contractual debt 
to its contractual partner – Mr Makate12 – in a contract whose terms were 
proved in the trial court and those factual findings were not interfered with by 
this Court in its 2016 judgment;  
12.3.2 Vodacom, a company now worth R200 billion,13 is also financially backed by 
an even larger giant from England: Vodafone’s 65% shareholding in Vodacom 
means that it is extremely unlikely that the payment of R9,4 billion would lead 
to Vodacom’s demise.  Vodafone has had no difficulty in settling prior disputes 
 
11 Vodacom’s application for leave to appeal (CC) at Vol 25 p 2584 para 65.  
12 This Court stated in its 2016 judgment (at para 104) “[t]he stance taken by Vodacom in this litigation is unfortunate. It is 
not consistent with what was expected of a company that heaped praises on the applicant for his brilliant idea on which its 
‘Please Call Me’ service was constructed. The service had become so popular and profitable that revenue in huge sums of 
money was generated for Vodacom to smile all the way to the bank. Yet it did not compensate the applicants even with a 
penny for his idea. No smile was brought to his face for his innovation. … it is ironic that … Vodacom invoked legislation 
from the height of the Apartheid era, to prevent the applicant from [continuing with legal claim against Vodacom].” 
13 Vodacom’s application for leave to appeal (CC) at Vol 25 p 2585 para 68.1.  
  6 
for amounts equal or beyond what Mr Makate is owed under the SCA’s order.14  
12.3.3 In truth, Vodacom’s complaint is that the contract (as proved) may amount to a 
bad bargain for it. But Vodacom received an excellent bargain:     
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
13 Sixth, Vodacom’s heads of argument impermissibly seek to expand the grounds of appeal beyond 
the issues and legal points raised in Vodacom’s application for leave to appeal.  
13.1 It is no answer for Vodacom to say that its application for leave to appeal stated that it was 
just giving a few examples.16 In every commercial matter, a party will apply for leave to 
appeal to this Court on the generic basis that the courts below were ignorant of the issues 
 
14 This was made clear in the disputes referred to in Mr Makate’s answering affidavit to Vodafone’s application to intervene 
as an amicus at Annex NM1 to Mr Makate’s opposing affidavit. 
15 Coppin J confirmed an agreement as testified by Makate.  He testified that he told Geissler that he wanted an “ongoing 
revenue share deal” - see Vol 17 p 1773-1774 lines 15-19, lines 22-25. 
16 Vodacom’s application for leave to appeal (CC) at Vol 25 p 2580 para 49.   
Under the SCA order, 
Mr Makate receives: 
Vodacom keeps: 
5% of the revenue earned from 
return calls after a PCM was sent 
95% of the revenue earned from return 
calls after a PCM was sent within first 
hour 
Only within the first hour 
(which is 27%) 
100% of the revenue generated from all 
calls made in response to a PCM for the 
remaining 23 hours 
Only in South Africa 19 other countries 
For 18 years15 Vodacom has already been using it for 23 
years and will continue to use it for so 
long as it generates an income 
No claim for income from 
advertisements 
All the money paid to Vodacom for 
advertisements. 
Mora Interest from 18 January 
2019 
 
  7 
and evidence, and therefore that their right under section 34 of the Constitution has been 
infringed. And thereafter purport to expand the issues in their heads of argument. This 
Court, as the apex court, has made clear (as we set out below) that it does not consider 
matters involving the incorrect application of established legal tests or a decision made on 
findings of fact.  
13.2 Vodacom’s attempt to expand the grounds and evidence on which it relies also causes 
significant practical prejudice in this matter. The record that serves before this Court is the 
same record that served before the SCA and runs to approximately 3000 pages. The parties 
agreed to the contents of the record on the basis of the points made in Vodacom’s 
application for leave to appeal. The High Court record, however, that served before Hughes 
J ran to approximately 7000 pages.17 Mr Makate has been asked to defend the order of the 
SCA (not the particular reasons) – it would have been open to him to include other aspects 
of the record before the High Court had Vodacom raised these points squarely and 
timeously.  
13.3 Indeed, Vodacom even seeks, impermissibly, to rely on some of the grounds and arguments 
that Vodafone wished to introduce – impermissibly –  under the guise of an amicus (such as 
the appropriateness of the application of substitution in cases of this kind). This Court 
rightly dismissed Vodafone’s application to be admitted as an amicus. Vodacom cannot 
seek belatedly to expand the scope of its application for leave to rely on those arguments 
and points.  
13.4 But, in any event, we demonstrate that the new features Vodacom raises are without merit 
on the basis of the evidence presented. This, again, highlights that the complaint that the 
High Court or the majority of the SCA were “ignorant” of the issues and evidence 
presented is unsustainable.  
14 Vodacom should be held to its case made out in the application for leave to appeal and those 
 
17 Mr Makate’s AA (CC) at Vol 27 p 2765 footnote 41.   
  8 
particular and specific features of the majority judgment that it raised as affecting the majority of 
the SCA’s order.  
THE LITIGATION HISTORY 
15 Mr Makate’s answering affidavit before this Court briefly summarised the history of Mr Makate’s 
struggle to be compensated by Vodacom at paragraphs 7 to 28.18 Given Vodacom’s ‘scorched 
earth’ approach, and the amount of issues that need to be dealt with in these heads of argument, we 
respectfully request that this Court have specific regard to those paragraphs, as well as 
Mr Makate’s founding affidavit in the review.19 
THIS COURT’S APPEAL JURISDICTION HAS NOT BEEN TRIGGERED BY VODACOM 
16 This Court may only entertain a matter that falls within the parameters of its jurisdiction as defined 
by section 167(3) of the Constitution.20 Vodacom must satisfy two requirements.  
16.1 First, the matter must fall within the jurisdiction of this Court.  
16.2 Second, the interests of justice must warrant the granting of leave.  
17 This Court has made clear that consideration of the interests of justice only arises at the second 
stage of the test. The interests of justice stage of the test “matters not if this Court lacks the 
authority necessary for entertaining the appeal”.21 
18 This Court’s jurisdiction is engaged if:  
18.1 The matter raises a constitutional issue; or 
18.2 The matter concerns an arguable point of law of general public importance that ought to be 
 
18 Mr Makate’s AA (CC) at Vol 27 p 2749 – 2755 paras 7 – 28.   
19 Mr Makate’s FA at Vol 1 p 9 – 27 paras 7 – 48.  
20 The Constitutional Court— 
(a) is the highest court of the Republic; and 
(b) may decide— 
(i) constitutional matters; and 
(ii) any other matter, if the Constitutional Court grants leave to appeal on the grounds that the matter raises an arguable point 
of law of general public importance which ought to be considered by that Court; and 
(c) makes the final decision whether a matter is within its jurisdiction. 
21 General Council of the Bar of South Africa v Jiba 2019 (8) BCLR 919 (CC) para 37.  
  9 
heard by this Court. 
19 We submit that Vodacom’s application falls short on both scores for two reasons: 
19.1 First, Vodacom’s application raises no constitutional issue, nor any arguable point of law of 
general public importance that this court ought to consider; 
19.2 Second, it is not in the interests of justice that Vodacom should be permitted to avoid fairly 
compensating Mr Makate for his idea, which it has used for over 23 years to generate 
billions of rands in revenue. 
20 Vodacom did not raise any arguable point of law of general public importance in its application for 
leave to appeal.22 If what is at issue in a particular case is “the determination of facts, the 
jurisdiction of this Court is not engaged”.23 As in Jiba – there was no mention of any constitutional 
issue by Vodacom before the High Court or the Supreme Court of Appeal.24 Vodacom claims for 
the first time in this Court that the matter raises a constitutional issue.  
VODACOM’S INTEREST OF JUSTICE ARGUMENTS ARE MISPLACED 
21 Vodacom attempts to repair the defects in its application for leave to appeal before this Court with 
its chapter on “the Interests of Justice”. But there are five fatal problems for Vodacom.  
22 First, this Court has made clear that the question of the interests of justice does not give this Court 
jurisdiction. It is a secondary question – if this Court has jurisdiction, then the interests of justice 
are considered to determine whether the matter should be set down.25  
23 Second, the evidence in the “interests of justice” chapter is entirely new evidence that Vodacom 
seeks to introduce belatedly on appeal before this Court. Vodacom makes no application in terms 
 
22 Section 167(3)(b)(ii) of the Constitution now empowers this Court to hear any other matter (i.e. a matter in which no 
constitutional issue arises) if: the matter raises an arguable point of law; of general public importance; which ought to be 
considered by the Constitutional Court. 
23 Jiba para 49.  
24 Jiba para 51.  
25 See Jiba cited above.  
  10 
of this Courts’ Rules to do so, and fails to show any exceptional circumstances.26  
23.1 Vodacom did not plead in its answering affidavit that the payment it would be required to 
make would have a substantial or devastating impact on it.  
23.2 The supplementary founding affidavit plainly explained what the capital amount sought 
under Model 9A was (for the period of 20 years) – approximately R9,7 billion. Vodacom 
has, therefore, known with precision – since the supplementary founding affidavit – what 
the capital amount Mr Makate was seeking was. There is no explanation regarding why the 
new evidence was not introduced.  
23.3 The allegations made are also speculative and rely on hearsay. There are no confirmatory 
affidavits from any of the protagonists mentioned.  
24 Third, the figures of R65 billion are, with respect, entirely sensational. But, in any event, the 
amount of compensation in a matter does not determine whether this Court has jurisdiction to hear 
the appeal. That is clear from the matters that this Court has correctly denied leave to appeal in the 
past.27  
25 Fourth, Vodacom belatedly calls into aid its shareholders who would lose money if the majority 
order of the SCA stands. But that is because for 23 years Vodacom and its shareholders have 
profited off PCM without ever paying its dues.  
26 Fifth, while the new evidence is disputed (and no application has been made to place it before this 
Court), the allegations cut against Vodacom. Vodacom explains that a payment of R40 billion 
would negatively impact Vodacom, but the amount due to Mr Makate under the majority of the 
SCA’s order is substantially less than R40 billion. It also demonstrates the huge amounts of 
revenue that Vodacom generates, and that Vodacom is entirely capable of paying the amounts due 
 
26 Rail Commuters Action Group v Transnet Ltd t/a Metrorail 2005 (2) SA 359 (CC) at para 41.  
27 Central Energy Fund SOC Ltd and Another v Venus Rays Trade (Pty) Ltd and Others (4305/18) [2020] ZAWCHC 164 
(20 November 2020) where the High Court ordered two state entities to pay compensation to foreign commercial entities 
in the amount of approximately 300 000 000 US Dollars; see also Passenger Rail Agency of South Africa v Swifambo 
Rail Agency (Pty) Ltd 2017 (6) SA 223 (GJ) where a private entity was ordered to return payments received in the amount 
of R4 billion, which led to the company going insolvent. 
  11 
in Mr Makate’s claim as ordered by the majority of the SCA.  
26.1 It pays dividends annually to shareholders in the amount of R13 – R14 billion and, even 
separate from that, Vodacom has sufficient funds available to spend a further R12 billion 
per year (R60 billion over 5 years) in network investment.  
26.2 The payment of Mr Makate’s claim would plainly not be the demise of Vodacom or its 
partners. At best, it might mean that Vodacom delays its “commitment" to improving its 
network for 1 year.  
27 Court orders in commercial matters often pertain to amounts of money that are significant. 
Moreover, Vodacom almost certainly would have undertaken insurance for the liability owed to 
Mr Makate and, indeed, if it did not do so then Vodacom’s shareholders may well have remedies 
against Vodacom’s directors for failing in their fiduciary duties.28  
VODACOM’S APPLICATION FOR LEAVE TO APPEAL: A VEILED APPEAL ON FACT  
28 Vodacom’s real complaint is that the majority of the SCA “erred in its evaluation of the evidence 
and determination of the issues before it”.29  
29 It is clear that what Vodacom seeks from this Court is an appeal against the factual findings and the 
re-evaluation of evidence by the majority of the Supreme Court of Appeal. That appeal does not 
engage the jurisdiction of this Court. This Court has made that abundantly clear in a series of cases.  
30 Thus, in order to style its application as engaging this Court’s jurisdiction, Vodacom claims that 
the majority judgment did not merely get it wrong, but so badly wrong that its orders “are 
unconstitutional”.  
31 Vodacom says so – principally – for three reasons.  
31.1 First, because the order made was beyond the Court’s jurisdiction.  
 
28 This point was squarely raised in Mr Makate’s answering affidavit before this Court (at Vol 27 p 2780 para 116) and 
Vodacom did not, for instance, seek leave from this Court to deliver any replying affidavit to dispute the point. 
29 Vodacom’s Application for Leave to Appeal (CC) at Vol 25 p 2566 para 8.  
  12 
31.2 Second, because the order is vague; and  
31.3 Third, because the orders made by Mocumie JA, Mothle JA and Nhlangulela AJA were: 
“the product of the majority’s ignorance or disregard of the true issues in the case and the 
evidence relevant to them”;30 and because the right to a fair hearing under section 34 of the 
Constitution protects litigants against “orders carelessly made in incomprehensible 
terms”.31 
32 We show below that each of these contentions is incorrect. The repeated criticisms – that the 
majority of the court was “ignorant”, “careless” and “disregarded” evidence and disputes between 
the parties – are unfortunate and entirely unwarranted.  
THE “JURISDICTION” POINT HAS NO MERIT 
33 Vodacom claims that the majority of the SCA exceeded its jurisdiction, because:  
33.1 it determined issues beyond the issues before it in Vodacom’s appeal; and  
33.2 a court that does so breaches sections 1(c) (the rule of law) and 34 of the Constitution (the 
right to a fair hearing).32  
34 Vodacom’s argument is based on two features of the majority’s order: substitution, and costs. The 
SCA order differed from the High Court’s order on remedy and costs but did not go beyond the 
appeal court’s jurisdiction.  
The complaint that substitution was not an issue before the SCA is unsustainable  
35 Vodacom’s suggestion that an order of substitution was beyond the issues before the SCA is 
unsustainable for six reasons.   
36 First, Vodacom attempts to gloss over the fact that Mr Makate squarely sought substitution before 
Hughes J. The basis for it was pleaded fully and canvassed before the High Court. Before the 
 
30 Vodacom’s Application for Leave to Appeal (CC) at Vol 25 p 2566 para 8.  
31 Vodacom’s Application for Leave to Appeal (CC) at Vol 25 p 2575 para 39.  
32 Vodacom’s Application for Leave to Appeal (CC) at Vol 25 p 2572 paras 30 – 32.  
  13 
Supreme Court of Appeal, Vodacom’s complaint with the High Court judgment by Hughes J was 
that the order - in effect - amounted to substitution.33 The issue of substitution, and its 
appropriateness, was thus fully pleaded and debated in the heads of argument and oral argument 
before the High Court and before the Supreme Court of Appeal.  
37 Second, a key issue of Vodacom’s appeal before the SCA was its complaint that the order by 
Hughes J amounted – effectively – to substitution. In Vodacom’s own words, the High Court:  
 “[O]rdered the CEO to redo his determination in accordance with its detailed directives. 
They are, in effect, a total substitution of the CEO’s determination, entirely subsuming 
his discretion”.34  
37.1 Vodacom complained that the “CEO will be hamstrung in being obliged to make his 
determination by applying the High Court’s formula”.35 Vodacom complained that under 
the High Court’s order the matter would be referred back to the CEO but “the CEO will be 
obliged to perform a mechanical exercise resulting in a new [sic] outcome”.36  
37.2 Vodacom argued that there would be no point in sending the matter back to the Vodacom 
CEO if the Vodacom CEO’s calculation is purely mechanical. All the majority judgment of 
the SCA did was agree, in part, with Vodacom’s argument and, in part, with the High 
Court’s reasoning.37   
38 Third, during the hearing before the SCA both parties were expressly asked to address the court on 
whether the court was empowered to depart from the order of Hughes J, if the court dismissed 
Vodacom’s appeal or whether the SCA was bound by the terms of the High Court order.  
38.1 It is, therefore, incorrect that Vodacom was “not called upon to address any suggestion that 
the SCA should replace the High Court’s orders”.38 Both parties had an opportunity to do 
 
33 Vodacom’s heads of argument (SCA) p 35 para 86.

RA p 1733 para 24, p 1735 para 26.  
  50 
157 The only fair and reasonable basis to calculate the effective rate is to use a blend between prepaid 
and contract rates, which should not be lower than the rates published by the regulator, ICASA. It 
can never be less than the ICASA rates, which are the rates for which Vodacom obtained statutory 
approval.  The effective rate used by the CEO was misaligned from even the lowest prepaid tariff 
and actual prepaid rate.220  The evidence demonstrates that the CEO used effective rates 
considerably lower than the ICASA rate.221 Moreover, as explained above, the CEO’s model was 
patently unreasonable and led to a patently inequitable result because of the exclusion of revenue 
derived from MTR as well as call-backs from contract subscribers.222  
Mora interest and time value of money223 
158 Vodacom’s counsel conceded during the argument before the review court a quo that mora interest 
on the amount decided upon by the CEO should have started to run on 18 January 2019 at a rate of 
10.25%. Mr Makate accepted that. Notably, Vodacom’s heads of argument provide for mora 
interest to run from an even earlier date (from the CEO’s Determination). There was therefore no 
reason to continue with further debate about interest. Again, the findings of the SCA majority are 
entirely unobjectionable on the issue.  
   
CONCLUSION 
159 For all of the reasons above, we submit that Vodacom has failed to make out any case for leave to 
appeal to this Court. We submit that Vodacom’s application should be dismissed with costs.
